speaker: interviewer
text: --of an interview with survivor Raymonde Fiol, conducted by Wendy Kreeger. So you arrive in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Totally overwhelmed [LAUGHS] because I had been so seasick for the 14 days that-- I was a little scared, too. The whole family-- I think it had snowed probably several feet, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just-- something like that. And all these people lined up at the pier. And I don't have my sea legs. So I mean, I'm just a wreck. My clothes are hanging from me because I had lost so much weight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband was with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We were on the ship, yes. And everybody was trying to greet me-- his father and mother and two aunts and his sister and his brother-in-law and two nieces. So everybody was, like, you know, I guess, probably a little apprehensive of meeting me, too-- and I, them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a little bewildering. I remember the day very well, even though they tried to make it very, very comfortable for me. And obviously my in-laws-- my mother-in-law was just a very, very warm lady. And we hit it off right, you know, right off the bat, yes. Had you shared your whole story with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [SOBBING] He'll see it on this tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you told him anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes. I mean, he knew my background and [CRYING] what had happened to me, but not in much detail. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, he gets pieces here and there-- not my children. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where did you go from the pier in Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we moved in with my in-laws temporarily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Bronx. And then we-- they used to have a bungalow in Coney Island for the summertime. So we went. And they rented a bungalow for all four of us. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came in '57, so the summer of '57. And then we got our own apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were how old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '57, 21. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Phil was 23, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two old folks. [LAUGHS] And we got our own apartment in the Bronx-- off the Grand Concourse, 183rd, very nice-- just a nice one-bedroom to get us started-- and did the usual newlywed things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he still in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He, he got out of the Army. And we proceeded to just run normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had been at, at CBS before he went to the Army. He was young himself. So he went to look for a job in sales. He didn't go back to CBS. And I went and got myself a secretarial job, same week-- got back, got here, and settled in into married life and newlyweds and getting comfortable with the country. I did not have a language problem, since I already spoke English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just joined the family-- my in-laws were very warm. They made a party for us to meet everyone. Phil has a sister and brother-in-law and two nieces. And I was taken in as a sister and a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed connected with the relatives in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In France.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Paris and in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever come here to visit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt and uncle came here in 1970. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What had happened-- I-- when I started to work, I worked for an airline. So it was easy for me to go back and fly back. But for a few years after, I had to start having children, it-- I couldn't go. And they didn't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children are David. David was born in 1960. And Ellen, my daughter, was born in 1964. I have two children. She is away in California, a little too far for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does David live?

speaker: interviewee
text: David lives in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's going to come--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --later with your husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for us to meet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they'll be here shortly. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to people, new acquaintances here, about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, once in a while, I do. People are fascinated. They want to hear more. I just have trouble, you know, continuing the story. [CRYING] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever communicate with the Cailacs again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sent them the wedding invitation. And they came. They came to my wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the first time you had seen them since you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it felt strange. It felt strange, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess it had been 10 years almost. And so it felt somewhat uncomfortable. But I'm glad they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you communicate with them from here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think for a couple years, we started, you know, sending cards to each other. But then I think they-- I think they were quite elderly. And I was never notified of their death. And that was the end of it. I wouldn't know even how to contact their daughter if she's still alive. So no, I really lost touch afterwards, yeah. It was probably-- you know, in a sense, on my part, I was angry at them for letting me go. So there's-- you know, was a little animosity probably on my part, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever told them that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think-- it's, it's not them. They had no power. I was taken from them. So-- it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why have you decided to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's important for [CRYING] the world to know. [SOBBING] And it shouldn't happen again. But it is, unfortunately. [CRYING] And for my family, maybe, it's one way I can communicate what I've gone through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's about 50 years since the end of the war, what kind of message might you have for your family and future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No war. No hate. It kills people. [CRYING] And the worst thing is the children. [SOBBING] People don't know what they do to each other. [SOB] Hate is no good. We're senseless. Because people have war, [CRYING] and it's the-- it's the children and the people who will suffer. It's leaders that fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what purpose? What do you accomplish? You don't resolve anything. So maybe one day will come when there is no war, no killings. What do you think about your childhood now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a childhood. [CRYING] [SOBBING] And when I speak, it's the child coming out of me. [SOBBING] I didn't have a childhood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's so important, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about that a lot when you were bringing up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried not to. I tried to block it out. I think, as a result, maybe an overprotective mother. But I think it's-- came with the territory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't mean any harm. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I get nervous. [CRYING] I get very upset if everything is not in order. I like to know where everybody is all the time. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's the result of my background. I know it, but I can't help it, you know? Yeah. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sometimes, you know, the kids don't like it, but-- especially a son. My daughter's more understanding. But-- Is there's anything that you weren't asked that you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think we've covered quite a bit, as far as I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you did a great job. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --we thank you for sharing your story with--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --us. We look forward to seeing your photographs--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and meeting your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you for a very interesting interview. You brought a lot out of me. [LAUGHS] I didn't think I, I remembered. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I believe this is my paternal grandfather. And his name is Mathias Nathansohn. The photograph was taken in Poland, definitely pre-war. I don't know the time. This photograph is of my grandmother and my father-- my maternal-- my paternal grandmother. Her name was Rachel Nathansohn. And my father is David Nathansohn. And it was taken in the '20s. I believe my father was 17 years old at the time. It was taken in Poland. This photograph is of my father, David Nathansohn, on the right, and my uncle, Gabriel Nathansohn, on the left. Taken also in Poland, but I, I don't know when. OK, this photograph is of my father, my mother in the middle, and my aunt, Paula. She's the one that took me in after the war-- and including myself in the carriage. Probably taken in 1936 or '37, I'm not sure. I look like a big fat baby here. [LAUGHS] OK, this photograph is of my parents-- my mother, Esther Nathansohn, my father, David Nathansohn, and myself, taken in Paris in 1937. This picture's of, of my mother and myself-- Esther Nathansohn and probably taken in-- I, I don't think it's in Paris. I'm not sure where it was taken. Maybe in 1941, '42-- I'm not sure. This picture is of the people who took me in during the war. On the left is Mrs. Cailac, in the middle, their daughter Evelyn, and on the right is Mr. Cailac and, presumably, their grandchild. I don't know where it was taken. It's definitely post-war because she was married after-- she was married during the war. This is a certificate that is put out-- was put out by the French government recording my mother being taken to Germany and subsequently to Auschwitz and just-- she never came back. This is a picture of my Uncle Leon, in England, and my Aunt Mary, and myself in the middle. And I believe I was 14 years old. So that had to have been 1950. In England-- I'm not sure where. This picture is of my Uncle Gabriel and myself on my wedding day. We were just going into the hall just before the wedding reception. It was taken in Paris in-- on October 7, 1956. This picture is of my husband and myself on our wedding day, taken in Paris, again, on October 7, 1956. Boy, we were kids-- [LAUGHS] our first dance. This picture is my daughter, Ellen Fiol. And it was taken in Maine, oh, maybe four years ago, a little more. She looks great. Too bad she's so far away. She lives in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, if you would introduce your family to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. To my right is my husband, Philip Fiol. And it'll be-- going to be almost 40 years of marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And to my left is my son, David Fiol, my little boy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Soon to be 36 years of sonhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Soon to be-- you're pushing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing your story once again. And thank you for joining her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for a wonderful interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: --this opportunity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really. [SIGHS] It's going to be nicely recorded for the world to see. Thank you.
